text:
RADIOLOGY REPORT

Patient: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [on file]
Study: Technetium-99m Methylene Diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) Whole Body Bone Scintigraphy WITH SPECT/CT of the Lumbar Spine and Pelvis; CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast
Clinical Indication: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), BRCA2 germline/somatic biallelic loss; currently on enzalutamide monotherapy (initiated approximately 6 months ago following progression on ADT + abiraterone/prednisone); PSA trending upward from nadir; surveillance restaging.
Ordering Physician: Medical Oncology
Comparison: Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 12 months prior (at 18 months on ADT + abiraterone), and prior bone scan at 12 months on initial therapy.

---

TC-99M MDP WHOLE BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT LUMBAR SPINE AND PELVIS

Technique: Planar whole-body anterior and posterior scintigraphic images obtained approximately 3 hours following intravenous injection of 25 mCi Tc-99m MDP. SPECT/CT of the lumbar spine and pelvis was performed to better characterize areas of interest. Images reviewed in comparison to prior studies.

FINDINGS:

Axial Skeleton - Spine:
The previously documented foci of increased tracer uptake at the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies remain present. Compared to the prior examination, these lesions demonstrate a predominantly sclerotic appearance on the correlative SPECT/CT images, with dense bony sclerosis consistent with the pattern of treated or healing osseous metastatic disease. There is no significant interval increase in tracer uptake intensity at either of these levels, and no new vertebral body lesions are identified throughout the cervical, thoracic, or lumbar spine.

Pelvis:
The previously identified focus of increased uptake at the right iliac crest is again noted. On SPECT/CT correlation, this area demonstrates dense sclerotic remodeling, with the osteoblastic character appearing similar to or slightly reduced compared to the prior study. The previously described right sacral ala abnormality continues to show sclerotic change with stable uptake. No new lesions are identified in the sacrum, pubic rami, ischium, or left iliac wing.

Long Bones and Appendicular Skeleton:
No abnormal tracer uptake identified in the bilateral humeri, femora, tibiae, fibulae, or bony thorax. No new focal lesions in the right or left femoral heads, necks, or intertrochanteric regions. The right femoral neck is specifically evaluated given the patient's recent report of hip discomfort -- no definite tracer abnormality is identified at this time, though clinical correlation is recommended if symptoms persist.

Ribs and Sternum:
No new foci of increased uptake in the ribs, sternum, or clavicles bilaterally. Degenerative changes are noted at several costovertebral junctions, unchanged.

Skull:
No cranial or calvarian lesions identified.

Other:
Physiologic tracer activity is seen in the kidneys and bladder. No superscan pattern. No soft tissue tracer uptake to suggest extraosseous disease.

---

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST

Technique: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following administration of intravenous iodinated contrast (portal venous phase). Oral contrast not administered. Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal reconstructions.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, pleural effusions, or consolidations identified. No endobronchial lesions. The airways are patent.
Mediastinum and Hila: No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with mild atherosclerotic calcification.
Chest Wall: No chest wall soft tissue lesions. Osseous structures of the thorax show no new lytic or sclerotic lesions beyond the known degenerative changes.

Abdomen:
Liver: The liver is normal in size with homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilation.
Gallbladder and Biliary System: Gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis or biliary ductal dilation.
Spleen: Normal in size and homogeneous attenuation.
Pancreas: No pancreatic masses or ductal dilation. The pancreatic parenchyma is within normal limits for the patient's age.
Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal. No adrenal masses or nodules, no adrenal metastases. Specifically, no adrenal enlargement suggesting adrenocortical involvement.
Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric in size with no focal cortical masses. No hydronephrosis. A small simple cortical cyst is noted in the upper pole of the right kidney, unchanged from prior. Mild cortical thinning bilaterally consistent with age-related changes.
Retroperitoneum and Mesentery: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. The great vessels are unremarkable.

Pelvis:
Lymph Nodes: The previously enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes are substantially smaller. The dominant node previously measured at 0.9 cm on the prior study (having decreased from 1.8 cm at diagnosis) measures approximately 0.7 cm in short axis on this study, representing continued interval reduction in size consistent with ongoing nodal response. No new pelvic sidewall, obturator, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Prostate/Prostate Bed: The prostate gland remains enlarged and heterogeneous. No new pelvic floor soft tissue masses identified.
Bladder: The urinary bladder is decompressed. No wall thickening or intraluminal filling defects.

Osseous Structures (CT):
The known sclerotic lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala are again identified. These are unchanged to minimally decreased in density compared to prior, consistent with the long-standing treated/healing response previously observed. No new lytic lesions are identified in the visualized pelvis or lumbar spine. No pathologic compression fractures. No new suspicious osseous lesions in the visualized portions of the proximal femora or sacrum.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Stable osseous metastatic disease. The known bone lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala continue to demonstrate a predominantly sclerotic, healed or healing character without evidence of new activity or interval progression. No new bone lesions are identified on this examination.

2. No visceral metastases. The liver, lungs, adrenal glands, and other abdominal and pelvic solid organs are free of metastatic disease.

3. Continued reduction in size of the right external iliac lymph nodes (now approximately 0.7 cm), in keeping with sustained nodal response.

4. No new intrathoracic, intraabdominal, or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

5. Mild right hip symptomatology reported clinically -- no corresponding scintigraphic or CT abnormality is identified at the right femoral neck or intertrochanteric region on this examination. Clinical follow-up is warranted if symptoms persist or worsen.

Note: PSA reported at time of imaging as 2.8 ng/mL, representing a mild upward drift from a prior nadir of approximately 1.9 ng/mL. Correlation with clinical and biochemical trajectory is recommended.

Dictated by: Radiology Attending, Department of Diagnostic Radiology and Nuclear Medicine
Electronic signature on file.
